author: @MikinessAnalog
text:
Actual experience with thermal runaway on some of the earliest versions of Li-ion made me hesitant to trust them for some time.
Then came the control circuit with temp monitoring, short protect, over & under volt protect.

author: @marcin4xm
text: of course it depends on the internal resistance of the battery. If the battery has a large c factor, it can convert more energy into heat in a shorter time. Such a cell is much easier to force to "do something".

author: @RSmerlinRS
text:
when I was young I had an old Motorola with a removeable battery.. after the phone stopped working I took the battery out, put it on top of a rock, and hit it with another rock.

INSTANTLY burst into flames. Was only ~1,000mah also, I think they've done some serious safety revisions for phone batteries over the years (especially since that Galaxy Note 7 fiasco)

author: @saifcathum3423
text: Nails don't work as good as screws and an impact driver. I theorize that the screw twists and locks the conductive layers together.

replies.author: @ablacknambercat
replies.text: And generates more heat due to the increased surface area and longer insertion time.

replies.author: @saifcathum3423
replies.text: @ablacknambercat Interesting point regarding surface area. I wonder if a coarse thread or a fine thread screw would preform better? If only there was someone with a large stockpile of batteries willing to test this theory.

author: @ahatton8553
text: Great video! I've just converted my power wheelchair to lithium batteries(Valence 12v) and I'm putting together an in-the-battery-box fire suppression system.  I've got about 7cubic inches of space to work with, and I'm debating using sand or pink roll fiberglass insulation to fill the space and maybe block oxygen by helpfully melting in case of a scenario. Would absolutely love to see a vid comparing the two if you have more phone batteries laying around ;)

replies.author: @totherarf
replies.text:
I can see a problem there. You will have no way to let the "normal" heat out of your battery pack .... giving rise to one of the key ingredients to a battery fire. Silica sand is used in large capacity fuses to quench the ark when the fuse blows, so it is a great suppressant material in that respect .... but you are not talking about suppressing an arc here! 
Obviously your use is a specialized one (you cant just walk away from it if it goes up) .... but maybe you should be looking at fire containment rather than suppression?

author: @Aldizerboer
text:
We own a scrap yard, in the last couple of years there has been a great number of scrap heap fires because of lithium ion batteries.

How I think they cause the fire in a scrap heap is that when a crane grabs a pile of steel scrap with a battery in it it get compressed and punctured there are multiple points in the battery where it short circuits.

I know no one is putting a blow torch on it because scap yards hate li-ion batteries.

Aside from that, I always enjoy watching your videos.
Please keep up the good work, this way we can always learn from each other!

author: @infinitelyuniversal2390
text: Not all chemistries are  equal, high discharge low internal resistance cells are more likely to go into thermal run away, especially Li-po cells and those are the ones you see more often go into thermal runaway

replies.author: @RaperJason
replies.text: Agreed like Robert said the Li ion ones are quite safe but Li Po are another kettle of fish 😂 Ive seen £1000 worth of Model heli totaled after a Li Po fire like a firework display

replies.author: @rogerstarkey5390
replies.text: All comparison in the auto application suggests that LifePo4 cells are FAR less reactive than other chemistries.

replies.author: @RaperJason
replies.text:
@rogerstarkey5390 
Lifepo4 is lithium iorn phosphate and agreed it is fairly safe
LiPo refers to lithium cobalt batteries as used in a lot of Model helis and you have to see one go to believe how hot they get 
The manufacturers usually recomend they are stored in a LiPo fire safe bag when not in use  
I know of one lad who was hospitalised just from the flash and fumes while dropping a crash damaged battery into a safe bag
The worrying thing is a lot of those small toy foam helis that were popular a while back were fitted with small lozange size LiPos and given to kids and as we know toddlers like to chew anything not a good scenario

replies.author: @infinitelyuniversal2390
replies.text:
@RaperJason totally agree i have used LiFePo4  for a year on my diy 12kw dirt bike  and purposely abused them, and could never get them to go into thermal runaway, but with only a 10C discharge rate and only 130wh/kg they are heavy chemistry and only give me 30km range with 1kwh battery.  
I have built another battery li-ion 180cell 20S 10P setup with samsung 30Q cells, that is about 2kwh 5C disharge rate gives me 60Km if it i ride it really slow 🐌  
My next built i would like to do Li-Po prismatic cells 20s 1P, my plan to keep cells cool is place aluminium sheeting to act as cooling fins between each cell and monitor with my smart BMS.
Then i want to make my own battery silver(+)-zinc oxide(-), potassium hydroxide(electrolyte) battery more energy density then gasoline per wh/kg and more power dense then Li-po,  high and low operating temperature ranges, environmentaly freindly, easy to make, no special lab conditions required, and only problem silver is expensive(but also an investment) and when recharging battery zinc dentrites like to form and short circuit cell, but i have ideas to get around that.
